does: This paper conducts participatory red teaming studies for text-to-image models across Ghana, Nigeria, and Indian regions (Karnataka and Punjab), prioritizing localized community engagement and training workshops to identify model failures through a cultural lens. The authors develop PLACES, a dataset of over 26,000 adversarial examples collected in partnership with local universities, revealing failure modes specific to non-Western contexts. Analysis demonstrates that safety vulnerabilities in T2I systems are shaped by local linguistic, cultural, and socio-cultural factors, with distinct adversarial patterns emerging from regional contexts that differ substantially from existing Western-centric crowdsourced datasets. The work emphasizes that model failures reflect culturally-specific interpretations of harm and normative boundaries rather than universal safety concerns.
connects: This work builds on the growing body of red teaming research for large language and vision models, which has traditionally centered on English-speaking, Western perspectives. It responds to documented critiques of AI safety frameworks that embed Western assumptions about harm and fairness while neglecting global diversity. The paper extends participatory AI research methodologies, which advocate for community involvement in AI design and evaluation, into the safety domain. It connects to scholarship on AI fairness and representation that demonstrates how training data and evaluation practices reproduce regional biases. The approach also builds on prior localization work in NLP and computer vision that shows performance disparities across languages and geographies, applying these insights to the underexplored domain of generative model safety.
enables: This work establishes a foundation for geographically plural approaches to AI safety evaluation and opens pathways for developing region-aware content filtering and harm taxonomies. The dataset and methodologies enable future research into culturally-contextualized safety metrics and prompt engineering techniques that account for non-English linguistic structures and cultural semantics. The participatory framework could be extended to other model types and additional geographic regions, fostering community-led safety research in underrepresented areas. The research points toward developing more locally-informed safety guidelines that reflect diverse cultural norms rather than imposing universal Western standards, and suggests mechanisms for iterative safety improvement that incorporate stakeholder feedback from deployment regions. It also enables investigation into how safety vulnerabilities vary systematically across linguistic and cultural boundaries, informing more robust and inclusive model development practices.
